# Overview {.unnumbered}

This book documents an end-to-end study of **bedload sediment transport inferred
from seismic noise** on the glacier-fed rivers draining Mt. Rainier, Washington,
during the **December 2025 Pacific Northwest atmospheric-river floods**.

It combines near-channel seismic stations (PNSN / Cascades Volcano Observatory)
with co-located USGS discharge gages along the Puyallup and Carbon Rivers, from
the glacial source toward Puget Sound.

## What's inside

- **[Manuscript](paper/paper.qmd)** — the research paper: setting, methods, results
  (the seismic–discharge scaling exponent and its frequency dependence), and
  discussion, with figures and the scaling table generated from the live data.
- **[Physics & code review](REVIEW_2026.md)** — a critical review of the
  Gimbert/Tsai forward models, the pipeline implementation, the corrected
  exponent baseline (it is **not** 5/4), the Tier-1 bug fixes, and the recomputed
  diagnostics.
- **[Literature & positioning](paper/LITERATURE.md)** — annotated bibliography and a
  novelty matrix against recent (2022–2026) fluvial-seismology work, with the
  reviewer objections to pre-empt.
- **[Reproducibility](workflows/README.md)** — the configs, workflow scripts, and
  commands to regenerate every figure and number.

## Headline result

Band-limited seismic power scales with discharge as $P \propto Q^{b}$. At the
glacial-source station the exponent **rises with frequency above the turbulent-flow
baseline** ($b\approx0.9$–$1.4$; @gimbert2014) — the signature of a bedload
contribution — while a lowland control river shows no high-frequency discharge
coupling. The honest, de-contaminated estimate is modest ($b\approx1.5$–$1.7$);
the larger values seen in early processing were artifacts removed by the pipeline
fixes described in the review.

::: {.callout-note}
This is a working research compendium. Sections flagged with 💬 are reviewer-defense
notes and open items, not final claims.
:::
